posts.draft:
# French Muslim Organizations Set Eid al-Adha 2026 for Wednesday, May 27

The French Council of the Muslim Faith and the Great Mosque of Paris have announced that Eid al-Adha in 2026 will be celebrated on Wednesday, May 27, marking the official date for France's Muslim community to observe the Islamic holy day.

## Background

Eid al-Adha, also known as the Festival of Sacrifice, is one of the two most important holidays in Islam, commemorating Prophet Ibrahim's willingness to sacrifice his son in obedience to God. The date is determined by the Islamic lunar calendar and the sighting of the new moon, which means it shifts each year on the Gregorian calendar. In France, Muslim organizations consult with international Islamic authorities and astronomical observations to establish the official celebration date for the country's estimated 5-6 million Muslims. The Great Mosque of Paris, France's largest mosque and a major institutional voice for French Muslims, typically coordinates with the French Council of the Muslim Faith to announce these dates publicly, allowing worshippers to plan their celebrations and prayers in advance.

## What happened

Both the French Council of the Muslim Faith and the Great Mosque of Paris issued announcements confirming that Eid al-Adha 2026 (corresponding to 1447 in the Islamic calendar) will take place on Wednesday, May 27. The Great Mosque of Paris released an official press statement setting this date, which was subsequently reported across major French news outlets including Libération, Le Figaro, Le Parisien, Ouest-France, and Beur FM. This announcement allows Muslim families, employers, and educational institutions across France to mark their calendars and accommodate the religious observance. The date designation follows the Islamic tradition of determining Eid celebrations based on lunar observations and astronomical calculations coordinated internationally.

## Reaction

Muslim communities in France welcomed the early announcement as it provides practical clarity for organizing celebrations, family gatherings, and religious services. The advanced notice allows employers to adjust work schedules and schools to accommodate Muslim students and staff observing the holiday. The announcement also reflects the institutional recognition that major French Muslim organizations receive in coordinating religious observances across the country, positioning both the French Council of the Muslim Faith and the Great Mosque of Paris as authoritative sources for Muslim life in France.

## Why it matters

Official date announcements for Eid al-Adha represent an important moment of institutional inclusion for French Muslims in the public calendar. While not yet a public holiday in France—unlike Christian holidays such as Easter and Christmas—the formal acknowledgment and advance notice by recognized Muslim institutions enables practical integration of Islamic religious observances into French civic and professional life. The transparency and institutional coordination demonstrated through these announcements contribute to broader recognition of Muslim religious practice as a legitimate part of French society. Such visibility also helps combat misconceptions about Islam in France by normalizing Islamic celebrations within mainstream public discourse.

---

**Why is the date different each year?**
Eid al-Adha is determined by the Islamic lunar calendar, which is approximately eleven days shorter than the Gregorian solar calendar. This means the date shifts forward each year on the Western calendar, attributed to both lunar cycles and the requirement to sight the new moon before declaring the official celebration date.

**Who decides the official date in France?**
The Great Mosque of Paris and the French Council of the Muslim Faith coordinate to announce the official date for France's Muslim community, consulting with international Islamic astronomical calculations and coordination with other Muslim-majority countries.

**Is Eid al-Adha a public holiday in France?**
No, Eid al-Adha is not currently an official public holiday in France, though Christian holidays and secular national days are recognized. However, the advance institutional announcements help employers and schools accommodate Muslim employees and students.

**What happens during Eid al-Adha celebrations?**
Eid al-Adha typically involves special prayers at mosques, family gatherings, ritual animal sacrifice (in adherence to Islamic tradition), and the distribution of meat to family, friends, and those in need. Many Muslims wear new or traditional clothing and exchange greetings.

Sources: Libération, Le Figaro, Le Parisien, Ouest-France, Beur FM, Actu.fr, Grande Mosquée de Paris

posts.summary: Eid al-Adha 2026: French Council of Muslim Worship sets celebrations for Wednesday, May 27
posts.draft:
The Great Mosque of Paris and the French Council of Muslim Worship have officially announced that Eid al-Adha in 2026 will be observed on Wednesday, May 27, marking the conclusion of the annual Islamic pilgrimage season and one of the most significant celebrations in the Muslim calendar.

## Background

Eid al-Adha, also known as the Festival of Sacrifice, commemorates the Prophet Ibrahim's willingness to sacrifice his son in obedience to God. The date is determined by the Islamic lunar calendar and follows the completion of the Hajj pilgrimage to Mecca. In France, the official announcement of Eid dates has historically been the responsibility of Muslim institutional bodies, particularly the Great Mosque of Paris and the French Council of Muslim Worship, which coordinate with astronomical observations and Islamic scholars to set the precise date for French Muslims.

The lunar Islamic calendar differs from the Gregorian calendar used in France, causing Eid dates to shift earlier each year by approximately 11 days. This variability requires annual confirmation by religious authorities. The designation of official celebration dates allows Muslim communities to coordinate prayer services, school closures, and public observances while informing French employers and government institutions about significant religious holidays observed by Muslim citizens.

## What happened

According to statements from the Great Mosque of Paris and reports published by Libération, Le Figaro, Le Parisien, Ouest-France, and Actu.fr, Eid al-Adha 2026 (1447 in the Islamic calendar) will take place on Wednesday, May 27, 2026. The announcement came from the French Council of Muslim Worship and the Grande Mosquée de Paris through an official communiqué released approximately 24 hours ago.

The official confirmation follows moon sighting observations and calculations performed by Islamic authorities responsible for establishing the Islamic calendar in France. This date aligns with celebrations occurring across Muslim-majority countries and communities globally, ensuring coordinated observance of the holy day. French Muslim institutions typically announce these dates several months in advance to allow adequate preparation for prayers, family gatherings, and religious services.

## Reaction

No immediate reactions from specific Muslim community organizations or civil society groups were included in the available reports. However, the announcement from France's primary Muslim institutional bodies—the Great Mosque of Paris and the French Council of Muslim Worship—represents the official guidance that French Muslims will follow for their religious observance. The advance notice provides Muslim employees, students, and families with sufficient time to request time off work or school and plan religious activities.

## Why it matters

The official announcement of Eid al-Adha dates carries practical importance for France's Muslim population of approximately 5-6 million people. Confirming the celebration date allows Muslim workers and students to coordinate with employers and educational institutions, reducing potential workplace or school conflicts. For Muslim communities, the institutional recognition of Eid by established French Muslim organizations reinforces their integration into French civic life while maintaining religious autonomy.

The role of French Muslim institutions in setting Islamic calendar dates demonstrates the balance between French secular governance and religious accommodation. By providing advance notice through official channels, these organizations facilitate better planning for public services, transportation, and commercial activity while affirming the legitimacy of Muslim religious practice within the French social fabric.

---

**Why does the date of Eid al-Adha change every year?**

According to Islamic calendar experts cited in background materials, the Islamic lunar calendar is approximately 11 days shorter than the Gregorian solar calendar used in France. This means Islamic holidays shift backward through the Gregorian calendar year annually, causing Eid al-Adha to occur on different dates each year.

**Who is responsible for announcing Eid dates in France?**

The Great Mosque of Paris and the French Council of Muslim Worship hold official responsibility for announcing Eid dates to French Muslims, as stated in their official communiqué. These bodies coordinate with Islamic scholars and astronomical observations to determine precise dates.
